# Blueprint v0.2.0 — first packaged release

Blueprint went from a quant catalog + sizing tool to an end-to-end
LLM optimization workflow for consultants and platform teams. This
release is the first packaged build of that workflow.

## What's in the box

### Custom calibration & quantization
- Calibrate tab with full `llama-imatrix` → `llama-quantize` → eval
  pipeline.
- Three bundled sample datasets (Stellaron Cloud, Apex Manufacturing,
  Vesper Indemnity) so you can walk the workflow in under 10 minutes.
- Pareto plot of quality / throughput / VRAM across calibrated and
  stock pre-quants.
- One-click client-ready report (`report.md`) with the headline
  "custom Q4_K_M beats stock by Δ%".

### Adapt
- LoRA training pipeline via Python sidecar (`transformers` + `peft`
  + `bitsandbytes` + `trl`). QLoRA on consumer 24 GB GPUs.
- Serve-time LoRA adapter loading (drop a `.gguf` adapter in
  `~/.blueprint/lora/`, pick + apply).

### Serve
- Three engines through one supervisor: llama.cpp (default), vLLM,
  TensorRT-LLM. Engine picker in the ServiceCard.
- Windows Service installation handled by this installer
  automatically; Linux systemd unit shipped as a separate script.

### Optimize at runtime
- Semantic prompt cache (TF-cosine similarity).
- Small-first model routing with escalation rules.
- LLMLingua prompt compression via Python sidecar.

### Operate
- Live GPU/RAM/CPU monitoring + per-GPU breakdown.
- Performance metrics scraped from `llama-server` / vLLM `/metrics`.
- Remote-LLM registry — monitor other Blueprint hosts or vendor APIs
  alongside the local one.

## Install

**Windows.** Download `blueprint-windows-amd64-installer.exe`, run
it (admin elevation required to register the service). Start menu →
Blueprint.

**Linux.** Download `blueprint-svc-linux-amd64` + `install-linux.sh`.
`sudo bash install-linux.sh` registers the systemd unit. Desktop UI:
download `blueprint-linux-amd64`, run.

**macOS.** Download `blueprint-macos-universal.app.zip`, extract,
open. (No service supervisor on macOS in this release — the desktop
app supervises its child directly.)

## Optional Python features

Once installed, the Dashboard's **Python runtime** card lets you opt
in to the optional Python stack:

| Feature | Adds |
|---|---|
| Python runtime (core) | +230 MB |
| Prompt compression (LLMLingua) | +250 MB |
| PyTorch + CUDA 12.x | +2.5 GB (NVIDIA only) |
| LoRA training pipeline | +800 MB (NVIDIA only) |
| vLLM engine | +1.2 GB (NVIDIA only) |
| TensorRT-LLM engine | +2.0 GB (NVIDIA only) |

All downloaded via `uv` on demand. No CUDA wheels in the installer.

## Known limitations

- Engine plan generation for TensorRT-LLM is not wrapped — Blueprint
  loads pre-built `.engine` plans you produce with `trtllm-build`.
- Calibration runs on consumer GPUs (8 GB VRAM) only work on models
  ≤ 7B. Larger models need 24 GB+.
- Windows installer is unsigned in this release; SmartScreen will
  show a warning. Signing certificate will land in v0.2.1.
- macOS service supervision is in-process only — the daemon-mode
  pattern hasn't been ported to launchd yet.
